content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI has completely messed up my sense of how difficult apps are to build.
Lately, I’ll find an expensive app that probably took a full team years to make and think, “I can probably build my own version.”
I do not spend enough time thinking about how long this will actually take. I just start. Lol.
Right now, I’m trying to make a medical education app where students can explore a 3D human body and virtually dissect it. Even typing that out makes me realize how ambitious it sounds.
There are so many parts I haven’t figured out yet. I need 3D models, accurate medical content, controls that don’t feel terrible, and a way to make everything run properly across devices.
Somehow, none of that stopped me from starting.
I think working with AI at Symph has changed my instinct here. We use ARIA to help us understand project context, investigate bugs, and explore different ways to build things. I’ve gotten used to approaching problems even when I don’t know exactly how to solve them yet.
So now, instead of asking how many months an idea might take, I tend to open a project first and worry about that later.
Probably not the best planning method, to be honest.
I can already tell that getting something on the screen will be the easy part. If I keep going, I’ll eventually need to think about whether the anatomy is accurate, whether students will find it useful, and whether I really want to maintain another app.
But those are future Drew’s problems.
For now, I’m still amused that an app idea this complicated no longer feels impossible. It feels more like something I can poke at, slowly figure out, and maybe turn into something real.
Or maybe I’ll just end up with a half-dissected 3D body floating on my screen.
We’ll see.
